title: Tuning with Validation Data
concept: Why we must use validation data to tune hyperparameter knobs, keeping the final test data locked away as an unbiased final check.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a major advantage of Random Search over Grid Search?

options.text: It is much faster and can test values in between fixed grid lines.
It is guaranteed to test every single possible combination.
It automatically cleans up noisy data labels.

exercises.explain: Correct! Random Search is faster and more flexible, allowing it to find sweet spots between predefined grid values.

cards.title: The practice exam
cards.body: Imagine a teacher who wants to test if students actually understand math. If the teacher lets students practice on the exact questions that will be on the final exam, the students will just memorize those answers. To test true understanding, students must study with a separate practice exam first, keeping the final exam a secret!

cards.title: Validation vs. Test Data
cards.body:
When tuning hyperparameter knobs, we divide our data:
• Validation Data: A separate "practice exam" used to test different settings and choose the best one.
• Test Data: The locked-away "final exam" used only once at the very end to check the model's true, unbiased performance.

cards.title: Trying on clothes
cards.body: Think of validation data as the fitting room mirror. You try on different outfits (hyperparameter settings) and compare how they look. The test data is the actual party. You only wear your final chosen outfit to the party once. If you kept changing outfits at the party, it wouldn't be a final choice!

cards.title: The three-way split
cards.body:
A standard project splits data into three separate buckets:
• Training Set (60%): The model uses this to learn parameters (like slopes).
• Validation Set (20%): The designer uses this to tune hyperparameters.
• Test Set (20%): Kept secret to calculate the final grade.

cards.title: Keep the test set locked
cards.body:
Myth: It is fine to use the Test Set to choose the best hyperparameter settings because it gives us the most accurate feedback.
Reality: If you use the Test Set to choose settings, your model "leaks" information from it. The model will look great during tuning but fail on real-world data because the final exam was no longer a secret!

cards.title: Validation vs. Test
cards.body: How the two evaluation datasets differ:

left.text:
Used for tuning knobs.
Checked many times.
Helps choose the best model.

right.text:
Used for the final grade.
Checked only once.
Must stay a secret during tuning.

exercises.prompt: Why must we use a validation dataset instead of the test dataset to choose hyperparameter settings?

options.text: To prevent the model from leaking test set secrets and keeping the final evaluation unbiased.
Because the validation set is automatically created by the model's weights.
Because the test set is only allowed to contain one row of data.
To make the training process take longer.

exercises.explain: Correct! Tuning on the test set causes information leakage, which makes the final score untrustworthy.

exercises.prompt: True or False: You should check the test set score multiple times while comparing different hyperparameter settings.
exercises.explain: False! The test set must remain locked and unseen until you have completely finished tuning and chosen your final model.

exercises.sentence: We use the training set to learn parameters, the ___ set to select hyperparameters, and the test set to get the final score.
exercises.explain: Correct! The validation set is specifically designed for testing different hyperparameter configurations.

exercises.prompt: Which of these describes the correct use of the test set?

options.text: Running the chosen model on the test set exactly once at the end of the project to report the final grade.
Using test set accuracy to decide whether to set a Decision Tree's max depth to 3 or 5.
Combining the test set with the training set to help the model learn its weights.

exercises.explain: The test set must remain completely unseen until the very end, serving as a single, final evaluation.

exercises.prompt: You try 5 different hyperparameter settings. Model A gets 90% accuracy on the validation set. Model B gets 92% accuracy on the validation set. You haven't looked at the test set yet. What should you do next?

options.text: Select Model B because it performed best on the validation set, then evaluate Model B once on the test set to get the final score.
Test all 5 models on the test set and choose the one that does best on the test set.
Combine both models and train them on the test set.
Ignore the validation results and pick a model at random to deploy.

exercises.explain: Perfect! Use the validation set to compare and select the best model. Once selected, test it exactly once on the locked test set to get the true final accuracy score. This keeps the evaluation honest and unbiased! You have completed the Hyperparameter Tuning unit!
